summary: Julia Evans describes the custom feedback site she built with Marie Flanagan to collect structured beta reader feedback on her zines. The original process of emailing PDFs and manually collating replies in Trello didn't scale beyond 10 reviewers. Inspired by the 'Help This Book' platform but needing image support, they built a site with five feedback categories: 'I learned something,' 'I love this,' 'I have a question,' 'I'm confused,' and 'I have a suggestion.' The categorized emoji system lets her quickly scan pages to see what's working and what needs rewriting, and she prioritizes feedback from learners over experts. The site has scaled feedback from 3-4 people to dozens and significantly improved zine quality.
key_points: Categorizing feedback before people write it guides them toward actionable responses and filters out unhelpful types like typo reports
The 'I'm confused' category was added after realizing that sometimes explanations are so unclear readers can't even formulate a specific question
Positive emoji signals (hearts and lightbulbs) are as valuable as negative ones because they show what doesn't need work and provide motivation
Feedback from learners is prioritized over expert suggestions, since the goal is teaching people who don't already know the material
The custom tool scaled the feedback process from ~10 people maximum to dozens of beta readers per zine
money_quotes: Sometimes an explanation is so confusing that the reader isn't able to formulate a specific question about what they don't understand.
Figuring out a specific question is hard, especially if the explanation you're reading isn't very clear!
As usual, actually writing the code was the easy part, so I'm not going to talk about that.
Being able to quickly scan through the zine and say 'okay, this has 10 hearts and 7 lightbulbs, obviously people love this page' is amazing.
We'll almost always prioritize 'I'm confused' or 'I have a question' feedback over 'I have a suggestion' feedback.
There's no category for 'this is a typo', because we don't want people to point out typos – that's the copy editor's job.
It's made it possible to get feedback from dozens of people instead of just 3 or 4, and I think it's really improved the zines' quality.
key_insight: Structuring feedback into predefined categories makes it both easier to give and easier to act on, turning an unscalable manual process into a systematic one.
